statusLine: Americas crude markets face modest pressure as WTI trades near $90 amid substantial US inventory draws and persistent Middle East shipping disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets showed mixed signals in the week ending June 6, with WTI falling 2.74% week-on-week to $90.54/barrel while commercial crude inventories declined sharply by 8.0 million barrels to 357.1 million barrels. This substantial inventory draw, well above typical seasonal patterns, suggests robust domestic refining demand as the summer driving season approaches. US production held steady at 13.7 million barrels per day, maintaining America's position as the world's top producer.

The $2.55 discount of WTI to Brent reflects typical Atlantic Basin dynamics, though the spread remains within normal ranges. Retail fuel prices provided relief to consumers with gasoline falling 16.6 cents to $4.44/gallon and diesel dropping 17.3 cents to $5.35/gallon, suggesting improved refining margins and adequate product supplies heading into peak summer demand.

Critical shipping disruptions continue to plague global energy trade routes, with multiple MARAD advisories highlighting Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait, plus Iranian threats in the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz. These chokepoints handle significant crude flows to and from Americas refineries, particularly on the Gulf Coast, creating ongoing supply chain risks despite current adequate inventory levels.

Across the broader Americas, production trends remain supportive with Guyana's Stabroek block continuing rapid expansion and Brazil's pre-salt fields maintaining strong output. However, Venezuela's production remains severely constrained under sanctions, while Canada's oil sands face ongoing pipeline capacity limitations that keep Western Canadian Select trading at substantial discounts to WTI.
keyPoints: WTI crude fell 2.74% week-on-week to $90.54/barrel in the week ending June 6
US commercial crude stocks dropped sharply by 8.0 million barrels, signaling strong refining demand
Retail fuel prices declined significantly with gasoline down 16.6 cents to $4.44/gallon
Multiple critical maritime advisories remain active for Red Sea and Persian Gulf shipping routes
US crude production steady at 13.7 million barrels per day, maintaining global leadership
Product inventories increased with gasoline stocks up 3.4 million barrels and distillates up 1.5 million barrels
